# Chapter 5. Over-Population

## 1. The "Iron Law of Wages"

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The Malthusians declare, as is well known, that the workers, as a consequence of their "frivolous habits," multiply more rapidly than the mass of available means of subsistence can grow, or, to speak more precisely, than the variable capital can grow. In this way it comes about that an overpopulation sets in, that more workers offer themselves to the capitalists than the latter can employ, that the available means of subsistence do not suffice for all the workers who exist, and that therefore, so long as the multiplication of the workers is not restricted, unemployment and hunger and all the vice and misery that follow from them are by natural necessity the lot of at least a part of the working class.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>So say the Malthusians. Let us now examine, with the guidance of Marx, how the reciprocal relations between the growth of capital and the multiplication of the working class actually shape themselves in reality.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">"The most important factor in this investigation," says Marx (p. 628 of the 3rd, p. 576 of the 4th edition of <strong>Capital.</strong> This discussion is missing in the 1st and 2nd editions), "is the <em>composition</em> of capital and the changes which it undergoes in the course of the process of accumulation."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">"The composition of capital is to be understood in a twofold sense. On the side of value, it is determined by the proportion in which it is divided into constant capital or value of the means of production and variable capital or value of the labour-power, the total sum of the wages. On the side of the material, as it functions in the process of production, every capital is divided into means of production and living labour-power; this composition is determined by the relation between the mass of the means of production employed on the one hand and the amount of labour required for their employment on the other. I call the former the <em>value-composition,</em> the latter the <em>technical composition</em> of capital. Between the two there exists a close reciprocal relation. In order to express this, I call the value-composition of capital, in so far as it is determined by its technical composition and reflects the changes of the latter: <em>the organic composition</em> of capital. Wherever the composition of capital is spoken of without further qualification, its organic composition is always to be understood."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">This composition differs among the various individual capitals. In what follows we assume the <em>average</em> composition of the social capital of a country.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>After these preliminary remarks let us proceed to our investigation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Above all we consider the simplest case: let accumulation proceed <em>without change</em> in the composition of capital, that is to say, a definite quantity of means of production always requires the same mass of labour-power in order to be set in motion. Let us assume, for illustration, a capital of 100,000 marks, consisting of three-quarters constant and one-quarter variable capital. If 20,000 marks of the surplus value are added to the original capital, then, under our assumption, the additional capital will be divided in the same proportion as the former; the total capital will now consist of 90,000 marks constant and 30,000 marks variable capital; the latter has grown in the same proportion as the former, by 20 per cent. But if the new additional capital is to valorise itself, it requires additional labour-power. The surplus value of 20,000 marks to be accumulated can, in our case, only become capital if the number of wage labourers at its disposal increases by 20 per cent.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If, with the composition of capital remaining the same, the wage labourers do not multiply as rapidly as it does, then the demand for workers grows faster than their supply, and wages rise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This is the case the Malthusians have in mind when, for the "solution of the social question," they recommend the restriction of the multiplication of the workers. In doing so, they overlook, first of all, that the capital relation, the relation between capitalists and wage labourers, is not abolished by the rise of wages. The accumulation of capital means the reproduction of the capital relation on an <em>enlarged scale,</em> means the growth of capitals and of the mass of surplus value, of unpaid labour, on the one side, and the <em>multiplication of the proletariat</em> on the other.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Even if the accumulation of capital raises the price of labour, this cannot happen without a simultaneous multiplication of the proletariat; it cannot happen without an extension of the domain of the rule of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Wages, however, can never rise so high as to endanger surplus value itself. Under the capitalist mode of production the demand for labour-power is called forth by the need of capital for its self-valorisation, for the production of surplus value. Capital will therefore never buy labour-power at a price that excludes the production of surplus value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If wages rise as a consequence of the accumulation of capital, then two things are possible: either the progress of accumulation is not disturbed by the rise in the price of labour – even if the rate of surplus value falls, the mass of surplus value may nevertheless rise at the same time as a consequence of accumulation. "In this case it is evident that a diminution of the unpaid labour in no way interferes with the extension of the domination of capital." Or accumulation slackens, "because the stimulus of gain is blunted." Accumulation decreases, but with it also the cause which drove wages upward. Wages consequently fall until they reach the level that satisfies the valorisation-need of capital. "The mechanism of the capitalist mode of production thus itself removes the obstacles which it creates."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We see here a peculiar reciprocal action between paid and unpaid labour.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">"If the quantity of unpaid labour supplied by the working class and accumulated by the capitalist class grows rapidly enough to be able to transform itself into capital only through an extraordinary addition of paid labour, then wages rise, and, all other things being equal, the unpaid labour diminishes in proportion. But as soon as this diminution touches the point where the surplus labour that nourishes capital is no longer supplied in normal quantity, a reaction sets in: a smaller part of the revenue is capitalised, accumulation flags, and the rising movement of wages receives a counter-blow. The rise in the price of labour therefore remains confined within limits which not only leave the foundations of the capitalist system untouched, but also secure its reproduction on a growing scale."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The fluctuations in the accumulation of capital, which hold wages within certain limits, appear to the bourgeois economists as fluctuations in the number of wage labourers offering themselves. They are here subject to a delusion, similar to that of people who believe that the sun moves around the earth and that the latter stands still. <a href="#ab3-kap5-n1" id="ab3-kap5-f1" name="ab3-kap5-f1">[1]</a> If the accumulation of capital slows down, this creates the appearance that the working population is growing more rapidly than usual; if the accumulation takes on a faster tempo, it appears as though the working population is decreasing, or growing more slowly than usual. In fact, as will probably be familiar to most of our readers, the phenomenon that wages fluctuate up and down without ever being able to overstep certain limits, the so-called "iron law of wages," is explained on the ground that, when wages rise, the working population as a result increases rapidly and the increased supply lowers wages, while a fall in wages results in greater misery and greater mortality in the working class, which diminishes the supply of labour-power and thus raises wages again.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Against this explanation there already speaks the simple fact that, as everyone knows, wages do not fluctuate from generation to generation, but at much shorter intervals. We shall return to this point.<br/> <a id="ab3-kap5-t2" name="ab3-kap5-t2"> </a></p>
```

## 2. The industrial reserve army

```{=html}
<p class="fst">We have hitherto assumed that accumulation proceeds without any change in the composition of capital. Such changes, however, necessarily occur from time to time in the course of accumulation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The <em>technical</em> composition of capital is affected by every change in the <em>productive power of labour</em>. The mass of means of production which a worker, under otherwise equal circumstances, transforms into product grows with the productivity of his labour. The mass of raw material that he works up grows, the means of labour that he employs grow, and so forth. With the productivity of labour, therefore, the quantity of means of production grows in relation to the labour-power incorporated in them, or, what is the same thing, the quantity of applied labour decreases in relation to the mass of means of production set in motion by it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This change in the <em>technical composition</em> of capital is reflected in turn in its <em>value composition</em>. It appears here as a relative decrease of the <em>variable</em> and an increase of the <em>constant</em> portion of capital. The changes in the value composition of capital do not, however, correspond exactly to the changes in its technical composition, since with the growth of the productivity of labour not only does the extent of the means of production consumed by it <em>rise</em>, but their value also <em>falls</em>, yet to a lesser degree than their <em>mass</em> increases. At the beginning of the last century, for example, the capital value invested in spinning was about half constant and half variable. The <em>mass</em> of raw material, means of labour, and so on, that a spinner today works up with the same expenditure of labour is many hundreds of times greater than it was then; the <em>value ratio</em> between constant and variable capital has, however, changed much less; the constant capital in spinning is now perhaps related to the variable as seven to one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In any case, however, the growth of the productivity of labour under the capitalist mode of production means a <em>relative</em> decrease of variable capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The productivity of labour and the accumulation of capital stand, however, in the closest reciprocal relation to one another.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Commodity production entails that the means of production are the private property of individuals. The development of the social productive power of labour, however, presupposes cooperation on a large scale, large workspaces, great masses of raw materials and means of labour, and so forth. The possession of such gigantic means of production in the hands of individuals is possible, under the rule of commodity production, only if individual capitals have been accumulated to a sufficient extent. “The soil of commodity production can bear production on a large scale only in capitalist form.” A certain height of the accumulation of capital is therefore the precondition of a certain height of the productive power of labour. Every method of raising the productive power of labour becomes, however, under the capitalist mode of production, a method of the heightened production of surplus value, and thereby makes possible a heightening of accumulation. This in turn brings about an extension of the scale of production, which again is the most powerful spur to a new heightening of the productive power of labour. The accumulation of capital and the productive power of labour thus develop each other reciprocally more and more.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The influence of the growth of the individual capitals through accumulation is counteracted by the simultaneous splitting up of old capitals, for example through divisions of inheritance, and by the detachment of new independent capitals. This counteraction against accumulation is, however, more than cancelled by <em>centralisation</em>, the <em>union of capitals already formed</em>, such as is brought about above all by the absorption of the small capitals by the large. This centralisation, like accumulation, likewise brings about a heightening of productivity, a change in the technical composition of capital. On the other hand, accumulation promotes centralisation and vice versa. The larger the capital I have accumulated, the more easily will it, in the competitive struggle, defeat and absorb the small ones. The more small capitals my capital has absorbed, the greater the productivity of the labour it keeps in motion, the more extensive the accumulation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The amassing of gigantic masses of capital in few hands develops, however, not only productivity in those branches of labour already subjected to the capitalist mode of production. A number of small capitals driven out of the great branches of industry are forced into branches of labour in which capitalist enterprise has not yet gained a firm foothold, where a small capital is still able to compete, and thus prepares the ground for the incorporation of these branches of trade too into the domain of capitalism.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus we see the capitalist mode of production caught up in a constant technical revolution, the consequence of which is a steadily advancing enlargement of the constant capital and a relative diminution of the variable capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And the relative decrease of variable capital advances incomparably faster than accumulation. The capital newly formed in the course of accumulation employs, in proportion to its magnitude, ever fewer additional workers. Simultaneously with accumulation, however, the revolutionising of the <em>old</em> capital also takes place. When a machine is worn out, then, if a technical advance has meanwhile taken place, it is replaced not by another of the same kind, but by an improved one, through whose employment a worker can deliver more product than before. The old capital is reproduced in an ever more productive form; this, however, has the consequence that it dismisses ever more of the workers it employed.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Centralisation is one of the most powerful levers of this transformation of the old capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more rapidly the centralisation and technical revolution of the old capital proceeds, the more accelerated must the accumulation of new capital proceed, if the number of employed workers is not to decrease. But the faster accumulation proceeds, the more centralisation and technical revolution are promoted.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The Malthusians tell us that “overpopulation” arises from the fact that the means of subsistence (or, more precisely speaking, the variable capital) grow in an <em>arithmetical progression</em>, in the ratio of 1 : 2 : 3 : 4 : 5 and so on, while the population has the tendency to increase in a <em>geometrical progression</em>, as 1 : 2 : 4 : 8 : 16 and so on. The increase of the population therefore always hastens ahead of that of the means of subsistence: the natural consequence of this is vice and misery.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What in reality advances progressively, however, is the <em>decrease</em> of variable capital, simultaneously with the <em>growth</em> of the total capital. The variable capital, if originally ½ of the total capital, becomes progressively only <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>3</small></small>, <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>4</small></small>, <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>5</small></small>, <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>6</small></small> and so on of the total capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">„This relative decrease of its variable constituent, accelerated with the growth of the total capital and accelerated more rapidly than its own growth, appears, conversely, as an always more rapid absolute growth of the working population than that of the variable capital or of its means of employment. Rather, capitalist accumulation constantly produces — and indeed in proportion to its energy and its extent — a relative, that is, an <em>excess working population surplus to the valorisation requirements of capital,</em> hence a superfluous or supplementary working population.“</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The change in the composition of the aggregate social capital does not proceed uniformly in all its parts. In one place capital grows through accumulation without this accumulation at first altering the given technical basis, and therefore takes up additional labour-power in proportion to its growth. In another place the composition of the capital changes without any growth of its absolute magnitude, merely through the replacement of old capital in a more productive form; — and the number of the workers employed falls both relatively and absolutely. Between these two extreme cases countless combinations occur, conditioned by the interaction of accumulation, centralisation, and the transformation of old capital into a more productive form, all of which have as their consequence either the direct dismissal of workers, „or the more inconspicuous but no less effective more difficult absorption of the additional working population into their accustomed channels of outflow.“ The working population is thus kept in constant flux, here attracted, there repelled, and this movement becomes the more violent, the more rapid the change in the composition of the capital, the greater the productivity of labour, the more powerful the accumulation of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Marx adduces several pieces of evidence from the English census for the relative, and often also absolute, decrease in the number of workers employed in numerous branches of industry. From more recent censuses we take the following two examples of an absolute decrease in the number of workers employed with a simultaneous expansion of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The one example shows us the <em>cotton industry of Great Britain</em> in the period from 1861 to 1871. In it there were</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <th> <p class="smc">The number of</p> </th> <td rowspan="5"> <p class="smc">    </p> </td> <th> <p class="smc">1861</p> </th> <th> <p class="smc">1871</p> </th> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="sm1">Factories</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">         2.887</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">         2.483</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="sm1">Spindles</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">30.387.467</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">34.695.221</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="sm1">Steam looms</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">     399.992</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">     440.676</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="sm1">Workers</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">     456.646</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">     450.087</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">We see, simultaneously with the decrease in the number of workers employed, a decrease in the number of factories and an increase in the number of spindles and machine looms; indications of a centralisation and accumulation of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>From 1895 to 1904 cotton consumption in England rose from 1,550 million pounds to 1,700 million, while at the same time the number of workers in the cotton factories fell from 539,000 to 523,000.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A similar picture is offered by several branches of the German textile industry: a strong decrease in the number of workers, which, however, is confined solely to the small-scale enterprises. The large-scale enterprises and their workers increase — thus a strong centralisation and accumulation of capital together with a simultaneous setting-free of workers. Thus we find, for example, in the <em>silk-weaving</em> industry of the German Reich:</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr valign="top"> <th> <p class="smc">Year</p> </th> <td rowspan="6"> <p class="smc">   </p> </td> <td colspan="2"> <p class="smc"><strong>Small-scale enterprises</strong><br/> (up to 5 workers)</p> </td> <td rowspan="6"> <p class="smc">   </p> </td> <td colspan="2"> <p class="smc"><strong>Medium-scale enterprises</strong><br/> (6 to 50 workers)</p> </td> <td rowspan="6"> <p class="smc">   </p> </td> <td colspan="2"> <p class="smc"><strong>Large-scale enterprises</strong><br/> (over 50 workers)</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><br/> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Enterprises</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Workers</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Enterprises</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Workers</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Enterprises</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Workers</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">1882</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  39.500</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  57.782</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">412</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">4.902</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  69</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  13.580</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">1895</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  16.527</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  20.484</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">192</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">3.169</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">140</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  32.121</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">1897</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">    8.272</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  12.823</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">346</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">5.650</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">240</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  48.719</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">Increase (+)<br/> or decrease (−)</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">−31.228</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">−44.959</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">−66</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   748</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">+171</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">+35.139</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Likewise in the linen-weaving industry:</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">1882</p> </td> <td rowspan="4"> <p class="smc">    </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  71.915</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  91.039</p> </td> <td rowspan="4"> <p class="smc">    </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  404</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">5.226</p> </td> <td rowspan="4"> <p class="smc">    </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">    73</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">    7.543</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">1895</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  34.082</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  43.228</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  291</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">4.598</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  120</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  19.966</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">1907</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  14.275</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  18.949</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  265</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">5.214</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  180</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  28.177</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">Increase (+)<br/> or decrease (−)</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">−57.640</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">−72.090</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">−139</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">     −12</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">+107</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">+20.634</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The number of workers in silk-weaving and linen-weaving together <em>decreased</em> by 60,540 in the 25 years, but the decrease was caused solely by the decline of the small-scale enterprise, whose establishments in both branches of production decreased by 88,868, by 80 per cent, while the number of their workers fell by 116,959. On the other hand, the large-scale enterprises increased from 142 to 420, their number almost <em>tripled,</em> and the number of their workers grew from 21,123 to 76,896, thus <em>by more than threefold.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have hitherto assumed that the increase or decrease of the variable capital corresponds exactly to the increase or decrease of the number of workers employed. This, however, is not always the case. If the manufacturer, the price of labour remaining the same, <em>lengthens the working time,</em> he will spend more in wages; the variable capital will grow, without more workers having to be employed – their number may even fall at the same time.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us assume that an entrepreneur employs 1000 workers, that the working day amounts to 10 hours, and the daily wage to 2 marks. He wishes to invest additional capital in his enterprise. He can do this in such a way that he enlarges the business premises, procures new machines and takes on more workers. But he can also employ the additional capital, in so far as it need not serve for the procurement of more raw material, in such a way that he lengthens the working time of the workers already employed. Let us assume that he lengthens it by 5 hours; that the price of labour remains the same; the daily wage will then amount to 3 marks, so that the variable capital will – other circumstances being equal – have risen by 50 per cent, without the number of workers having grown. But every capitalist has an interest in achieving an increase of labour rather through the lengthening of the working time or the enlargement of the intensity of labour than through an increase of the number of workers, since the amount of constant capital which he has to lay out grows much more slowly in the former case than in the latter. And this interest is all the stronger the greater the scale of production. Its force therefore grows with the accumulation of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If, for example, the worker's means of labour is a spade costing 2 marks, the entrepreneur will scarcely resist very much achieving an increase of labour through a corresponding increase in the number of workers. It is otherwise when the worker applies a machinery costing 100,000 marks.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But with the accumulation of capital there grows not only the striving of the capitalists to achieve an increase of labour without a corresponding increase in the number of workers; with it there also diminishes the power of the working class to offer resistance to this tendency. The surplus quantity of workers produced by the accumulation of capital reduces, through its competition, the power of resistance of the employed workers. These are thus compelled to submit to overwork; and the overwork in turn swells the ranks of the superfluous working population. The unemployment of the one conditions the overwork of the other, and vice versa.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We see that the accumulation of capital, with its accompanying phenomena and consequences – the centralisation of capitals, the technical revolutionising of the old capital, overwork, etc. – has the striving to <em>reduce</em> the number of <em>employed</em> workers in proportion to the total capital applied, and sometimes even absolutely.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But at the same time it <em>increases</em> the number of the workers who <em>offer themselves,</em> who stand at the disposal of capital, to a degree that far exceeds that of the increase of the population in general.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have seen in the second section how manufacture, and still more large-scale industry, in the course of their development make unskilled labour-power usable in place of skilled; the worker's period of apprenticeship shrinks to a minimum, the worker is put in a position to be applied by capital earlier, the time of his reproduction is shortened. At the same time adult male workers are rendered dispensable in many branches of labour by women and children. Thereby not only is the workers' army immediately enlarged to an enormous degree; the economic independence of girls and young people, their working together, as well as the possibility of being able to let the children earn along with them in early youth, promote early marriages, and thus likewise shorten the reproduction time of the working class.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A further powerful cause of the rapid swelling of the workers' army comes into effect as soon as the capitalist mode of production takes possession of <em>agriculture.</em> Here the increase of productivity brings about from the outset not merely a proportional but also an absolute decrease in the number of employed workers. In Great Britain the number of those employed in agriculture amounted in 1861 to 2,210,449, in 1871 to only 1,514,601, a decrease of almost 700,000. Those thus rendered "superfluous" move into the industrial districts, in so far as they do not emigrate altogether, and increase there the workers' army that offers itself to capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us finally not forget the effect of the railways and steamships, which enable capital to draw new masses of workers from industrially backward regions – Irishmen, Poles, Slovaks, Italians, Chinese, and so forth.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus the working population increases exceedingly rapidly, more rapidly than the need of capital for labour-power to be applied, and the consequence is a relative <em>overpopulation</em> which, as we have seen, is produced by the accumulation of capital; not by the increase of the <em>unproductivity</em> of labour, and the consequence is a relative <em>overpopulation</em> which, as we have seen, is produced by the accumulation of capital; not by the increase of the <em>unproductivity</em> of labour, as the economists maintain, but by the growth of its <em>productivity.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The existence of a so-called overpopulation, the presence of an <em>industrial reserve army,</em> does not, however, hamper the development of capital, but constitutes, from a certain point onwards, one of its preconditions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Capital is, as we know, an elastic magnitude. The more the capitalist mode of production develops, the more violent and more extensive become its periodic expansions and contractions. Modern large-scale industry moves, as already indicated in the second section, in a cycle peculiar to it, which up to 1873 repeated itself in periods of about ten years; it begins with a middling liveliness of the course of business, this grows rapidly, an economic upswing sets in, a colossal sudden expansion of production, a production fever – then the crash, the stagnation of business life, until the markets have expanded correspondingly and absorbed the surplus of products, whereupon a recovery sets in and the old game begins anew on an enlarged scale.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>So it was when Marx composed his <strong>Capital,</strong> which first appeared in 1867. So it was when he wrote the <em>Afterword</em> to the second edition of his <strong>Capital</strong> (on 24 January 1873), in which he declared that the general crisis was on the march. <a href="#ab3-kap5-n2" id="ab3-kap5-f2" name="ab3-kap5-f2">[2]</a></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We all know how soon and only too exactly this prophecy became truth.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>With the crisis that began in 1873, however, the capitalist mode of production seemed to have entered a new phase. If the productivity of large-scale industry had until then developed so rapidly that at times it grew faster than the expansion of the world market, now, as a consequence of the colossal advances of technology and the enormous extension of the domain of capitalist production – as far as Russia, America, the East Indies, Australia – the time seemed to have come when the world market was able to absorb the products of world industry only temporarily and exceptionally: instead of a cycle of ten years, in which moderate liveliness of economic life, feverish production swindle, crash, stagnation, and revival alternated with one another, we have had since 1873 the chronic stagnation of business, the enduring stagnation in the economic sphere, which was interrupted only in 1889 by an improvement in the course of business, a brief flaring-up of the spirit of speculation, which soon passed again and made way for an even worse stagnation of economic life. It seemed as though a more significant "economic upswing" would never come about at all. This assumption was, however, mistaken. From 1895 to 1900 we had once again a period of economic upswing, an upswing so vigorous that it even seduced not a few optimists into the opposite assumption, namely that the era of crises was over altogether or that these were in decline.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This assumption was untenable from the outset, since an economic upswing under the capitalist mode of production must necessarily end in a crisis, which indeed promptly ensued.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In this connection, however, only the temporary expansions and contractions of capital matter, and such take place during the chronic stagnation of business just as much as in the ten-year cycle of crisis and economic prosperity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Each such temporary expansion of capital creates a greater need for labour-power; how is this met? Wages rise, and according to the theory of the economists this results in an increase of the population – after twenty years the working population will have become numerous enough for capital to exploit the favourable conjuncture. But this lasts each time only a few years, often only a few months! Fortunately for capital, the actual state of affairs is different from what the theory of the "iron law of wages" would have it. The capitalist mode of production, as we have seen, artificially produces a surplus working population; and this is the <em>reserve army</em> from which capital can at any moment draw as many additional workers as correspond to its needs; without it the so peculiar spasmodic development of capitalist large-scale industry would be impossible. Where would German industry be if, at the beginning of the seventies and likewise in the second half of the nineties, it had not found so many hands that were "free" and stood at its disposal, whole armies of workers whom it could throw onto railway construction, into new coal pits, ironworks, and so forth. But this reserve army not only makes possible the sudden expansion of capital, it also presses down on wages, and since it is scarcely fully called upon even in the times of most flourishing business, it works so that even at the time of the greatest production din wages cannot rise above a certain level.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What appears as an up-and-down fluctuation in the size of the population is in truth only the reflection of the periodic expansion and contraction of capital. When the Malthusians demand of the workers that they should regulate their multiplication according to the degree of employment available to them, this means nothing other than that they should adapt their numbers to the current needs of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Malthusianism rests on a confusion of the so variable production needs of capital with the productive power of the existing means of production; if this confusion was always absurd, it became most manifestly so in the last two decades. Since then we have had in the flat countryside of Europe an overpopulation arising from a superabundance of means of subsistence, an overpopulation as a consequence of American, Indian, and Australian meat and bread competition!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Absurd as this may sound, the demands of Malthusianism are nevertheless only the corresponding expression of the position which the worker today occupies in relation to capital: he is only an appendage of capital; during the production process the means of production employs him, not he the means of production; but he also belongs to capital outside of labour, as we have seen; when he consumes, when he maintains and reproduces himself, he must do so in the manner that best corresponds to the interests of capital. His own product subjugates the worker: it makes not only his labour-power serviceable to itself, but all the activities of his human being.</p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ab3-kap5-f1" id="ab3-kap5-n1" name="ab3-kap5-n1">1.</a> Marx says:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">"Thus in the crisis phase of the industrial cycle the general fall of commodity prices is expressed as a rise of the relative value of money, and in the prosperity phase the general rise of commodity prices as a fall of the relative value of money. The so-called currency school therefore concludes that with high prices too little, and with low prices too much money circulates. Its ignorance and complete misapprehension of the facts find a worthy parallel in the economists who interpret those phenomena of accumulation to mean that at one time too few and at another time too many wage labourers exist."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ab3-kap5-f2" id="ab3-kap5-n2" name="ab3-kap5-n2">2.</a> Dr. Stegemann, whom we have already characterised in the second section, remarks with a shudder in reference to this sentence: "<em>Marx has no scruple</em> (!) in announcing the general crisis as imminent." <strong>(Preußische Jahrbücher,</strong> LVII, p. 227) Marx speaks in the passage in question of "the vicissitudes of the <em>periodic cycle</em> through which <em>modern industry</em> runs and whose <em>culminating point</em> – the general crisis." One can hardly speak more clearly. This does not prevent the learned Herr Doctor from construing the crisis of which the discussion is – as the <em>revolution</em>. Similar "confusions," to put it parliamentarily, naturally always in favour of the most gruesome interpretation – have befallen all too many "scholars" who have read – or also not read – and cited Marx.</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: Chapter 4. The Transformation of Surplus Value into Capital](ab3-kap4.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd) [Next: Chapter 6. The Dawn of the Capitalist Mode of Production ↓](ab3-kap6.qmd)
